sentences.text: My name is Max Stodel, spelled S-T-O-D-E-L.
I am born in Amsterdam, the Netherlands, on April the 12th, 1923.
My father's name was Isaac.
He was-- yeah, very difficult to translate.
When they slaughtered the cows, the hide came off.
And he put some stuff on it to prepare for making leather of it.
That what they call it in English?
Fine with me.
Oh, yeah, because I was with father, I think, the last day before he died because we were on same transport sent to Germany.
Oh, as a child, I remember everything.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
My father was, for me, a figurehead.
And I had a good rapport because family life in Amsterdam, in the Netherlands, was very good.
Very, very good.
I was the youngest of seven children.
And I was the baby.
So I don't think I was spoiled, but I think it was a little bit different than, let's say, the middle sister, middle-- one of the middle children or the oldest.
Because my mother was 49 years old when I was born.
And I think it is-- for the age, it's very, very old.
And so like I said, my father, my mother owned this-- you can decide.
But it was a very, very, very good.
Absolutely, there was no divorce in our family, there was no fights.
And a real family life, real, real family life.
Oh, I was a soccer player.
And-- and when I was playing soccer, not knowing that my father was-- so cared for me that he even came-- he went out of his way to come to the field to see me play.
And he bought me candy to-- because he was a real family man, you know.
And I don't know the difference between bad and-- and good because it was only bad, only bad in a sense that I might have been spoiled.
But it was very good for me that he did-- that he was like he was.
Like my mother, the same way.
Betje.
Her maiden name was spelled S-C-H-A-A-P.
Schaap.
No, she was always a housewife.
I had two brothers and four sisters.
So that made seven with me.
Oh, sure I can name them.
If you want me to, I'll go ahead.
My oldest sister, her name was Vrouwtje.
And I'm calling them by their full names now.
Then my next sister was Rachel-- Shelly, we called her.
My next one was Eva.
Then was Clara.
And then I had a brother by the name of Josef.
We called him Jopy.
And brother-- my youngest brother was Abraham-- we called him Abram.
Oh, I went to Talmud Torah, a Jewish school.
Only Jewish people.
Only Jewish people.
I'm going to show you later on, if you are interested, a picture that was taken in 1936 that we were out on a field day.
For three days, we went to the train and everything.
I'm going to show you a picture with the date and everything on it.
My oldest brother and my youngest brother, my two brothers then, they went both to Talmud Torah.
And my oldest-- my sisters, I don't recall that they went especially to Talmud Torah.
I think they went to school because before I went to Talmud Torah, when I lived in other neighborhood-- that was up to 1929-- I was born in 1923.
I was in a public school.
But after school, I had to go to Jewish school for an hour a day, or an hour and a half a day.
And I just want to go a little further with Talmud Torah.
Talmud Torah was a Jewish school.
In winter time, we went home 12:30 on Friday.
But we worked every-- we went to school every year Sunday on account of Shabbos was no school.
I did occasionally because my teacher was a chazzan in the temple, in the synagogue, Yiddish temple.
And he kept a list.
And he asked the people on Sunday morning, did you go Friday night, Saturday morning, or Saturday afternoon to shul?
And he kept track of it.
And since he was the chazzan there, so he knew everybody was coming into the temple, so I couldn't lie.
And I was going to his shul.
I couldn't lie.
Yes, I was.
If he was there himself, so I couldn't to say yes if the answer was no, you know.
No, I don't think I as much as I did because like I said, I was in his class.
And he could take me down if I went than they did.
Because I was not the same timings in Talmud Torah when my oldest brother was in because he was older.
I think my oldest brother was born in 1914, if I'm not mistaken.
So he was nine years older than I am.
No.
My mother was raised in an orthodox family.
And even-- I can see it in front of me that my mother inherited the dishes for Pesach.
We had different-- she was kosher.
She was kosher.
But when my father was out of work sometimes, and the kosher meat and the kosher stuff was a little higher than the regular, she couldn't afford.
Not that she did eat chozzer-- far from it.
She ate then-- she had meat and butter and whatever from a Jewish store.
But not kosher, but not treif, either.
Yeah.
My father, he stayed in bed and home for most of the day but Yom Kippur.
He didn't go to shul.
He fasted.
So did my mother.
The light just dimmed.
And we didn't-- and we had to-- when it was the day of Yom Kippur.

The day of Yom Kippur, then we ate late at night, as you all know that it is customary to eat, I think it was herring at the time, and cheese and special kind of bread, and all that kind-- I don't have to-- that's the way that life was with the family.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
I had uncles and aunts and nephews and cousins, you know.
And-- but if you ask me-- if you're going to ask me, I volunteer it to you.
I can't remember.
I don't.
I don't-- I'm almost 90%-- 100% sure that none of my aunts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe one of two cousins, came back-- who I spoke and now I remember.
But from a family, my closest family was 18 people, children and in-laws.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only one of my family came back.
And that is the youngest child of my youngest-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who is still alive.
Absolutely.
I was named after my uncle.
And that didn't a week go by or my mother made me go to visit him.
I what?
Yeah, my father's sister was never married.
And she was a single maiden.
And she went from sister to brother, because she had no family.
And then one day, it was my father's turn to take his sister in for a while.
And that while was till she passed away.
And there's a story to that, the way she passed away also.
I mean, she passed away-- passed away-- she was killed in Auschwitz, or whatever.
And I might have it on paper where she was-- yeah, I have it on paper where she was killed.
No, my mother died in 1939 around Purim.
And she's buried in the Netherlands.
And I-- I know exactly.
She was a sick person.
She was in the hospital.
And she always came home.
After one week when she was home, she died while I was laying in bed.
She fell off-- she was sitting on the side of the bed, and I heard her fall off the bed.
My father and me were the only ones in the house.
[CRYING] And I heard her fall, and fell dead.
I was at the time 15 years of age.
I think it was '39.
I was born in-- I was about a month before I would turn 16.
I was still 15 at the time.
I don't want to put my sisters and brothers down.
But I was the only one who mourned for a whole year.
[CRYING] And I was a soccer player.
And I couldn't do without it.

So I went-- that is customary in the Netherlands, if you want to do something that you're not supposed to do while you were mourning, I went to the-- to the-- to the rabbi-- the highest in the-- in the city.
And I told him that I was mourning for a year, and I wanted to play soccer.
He said, since it is a physical thing, he let me play soccer.
Maybe so, because I learned in school that you have to mourn and be sitting-- all of my family was sitting Shiva for seven days.
And but I was the only one.
And I think my father also mourned, really mourned for a year.
My mother died in 1939.
My sister-- my aunt, my father's sister, was living with us.
And my mother had a rheumatic arm, and she couldn't do the chores for the house.
So my aunt did it.
And she kept doing that, naturally, till [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she went to the camp.
But maybe I go a little further than you ask me.
But I'm going to come on back later onto how my aunt died, what I said a second ago, also.
That was--I listen to what happened in Poland, and all those countries where anti-Semitism was very-- really minuscule.
It was just a way of life.
We lived between the Gentiles.
If you didn't live where I lived in Amsterdam, there is no comparison.
You can't visualize what it is to live in Amsterdam between Jews and Gentiles because you got bad ones, you got good ones.
Jewish, you got bad one and good.
And every nationality, I don't care how prejudiced you are, you have to admit that there are always bad ones and good people in every religion, in every race, in every color.
And nobody can deny that.
No, really it was just a way of life, you know.
If they give you a nickname, that was one ear in and one ear out.
You know?
And the next day or the same day, you played with the same guy.
Because you expecting it, so it was nothing unexpected, you know.
Oh, what changed then?
The family got closer together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, boy.
That-- that was something, because my sisters and my brothers, even-- even though they had families, and it became-- like, to my father, because, like I said, my mother died right before war.
We were protecting over each other.
It was unbelievable-- unbelievable.
No, because everybody, every Dutch people was hurt by that invasion, what they came in there, because the way they came in.
Sure, then there were people who turned their back on the Netherlands and supported the Germans.
And they even squealed on people who worked for the good of the Netherlands, for the Jews.
And like I said before, there's always bad people.
There's no denying that there are always bad people-- profiteers, you know, who wants to make money, and get a kick out of getting even with some hatred, you know, and jealousy.
I was 14 years of age.
Let me see.
It was in '40.
It was-- I was born in -- I was 17 years of age.
I was working at the time, yeah.
I was a bursar.
I was in men's clothes at the time.
No, because my boss-- my bosses, two bosses, I had.
One, her name was Susan, and the other one was Pozarowski-- a Polish-- a Polish man.
His daughter might be still alive in the Netherlands.
And maybe she lives in Israel now.
But I heard from her once.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And the garment industry, where I was working at the time, was 95%, 98% Jewish people.
And it was in a Jewish neighborhood, also.
That changed when I was sent to a camp in the Netherlands in 1942.
On April the 1st, I was supposed to go.
But I got married that day because in Holland, you got the pre-marriage 14 days before you married.
And I married on April the 1st.
And April the 2nd, I was by the first train out, sent to a camp by the name of Kremboong in the Netherlands.
There was a dance.
My first wife-- there was a dance in the Netherlands.
And there were only Jews, because we got more close together when the Germans came.
And she was still as a single person.
I was a single person.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took her home that night from the dance.
And one thing to another, we got engaged.
And then we got married.
The Jews were in danger.
But it didn't start-- the real danger didn't start till '42.
And my second wife's father was one of the first ones who was picked up from the street and sent away to wherever.
I think I got it on paper here where he died, and when he died, and how old he was, and when he was born, and all that.
In 1934 or '33 when the Germans came to-- the Jewish Germans came to Amsterdam, because there was trouble in Germany.
Even a blind man could have seen that danger is coming.
I mean, they were up to no good.
1933, They came-- I would say 90% of the Jewish Germans who left Germany came to the Netherlands.
First of all, it was the closest by country.
There's only one border they have to cross.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they settled down in the Netherlands.
And as you well know, you might know, that they even became Dutch citizens, because the Netherlands is well known for taking in people who are looking for exile.
My sister was a men's tie maker.
And one of her bosses-- one-- a German guy-- a German-- I think it was a German guy who-- he had a factory.
And my-- my-- my wife-- my sister worked for him.
You know, he made-- she made the ties for him.
And I, for one, when my sister got sick, I was 11, 12 years old, when I was sitting off the sewing machine.
Oh, yeah, but I was too young at the time to get into politics. 16 years, was more thinking about girls than politics.
I had a job.
And when I was taken to the camp, I had no choice.
I was not taken to the camps-- I was sent to the camp, because I did-- I went to the Dutch camp.
I had to go on my own to the camp.
They told me I had to report April the 1st.
I had to leave Amsterdam to go to north of the Netherlands.
And I had to take a train.
I'll never forget the time.
The train I needed was April 2.
And the train left 10 minutes after 7:00 in the morning.
Kremboong.
No.
This was only for men.
Only men.
All ages.
All ages, because we had to work there.
And there was only one man named by the same family name, and even though he was not related to me.
What I did in Kremboong?
There was so much land there, we had to dig in it and to make it fruitful that it can make seeds, some whatever, corn, potatoes.
I don't know what it was.
It was just a springboard for labor.
I was in Kremboong from April the 2nd, 1942 till the middle of July, or end of July something.
It was in July, yeah.
Oh, and we-- while we was there-- while we were there-- I wouldn't have thought it, even though you didn't ask me for it.
When I left in April 2, we didn't wear David stars yet at the time.
And then there come a law that you had to wear David stars.
So everybody in the camp had to sew on a David star.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were-- there was an order we have to go to Westerbork.
I was there in April.
Left in July.
So it must have been end of May or June.
I don't know.
Either one of the two months because I know I was there for a while.

And after that, the star was there for a while, even though when I came back, I got one day-- because my wife was sick in the hospital-- I got one day from the camp that I was-- I was in Westerbork at the time.
Yeah, that was in Westerbork.
One day, I was allowed to go to see my sick bride in Amsterdam.
And when I put on suit in Amsterdam, I left the house to go to the hospital without a David star.
And I had to go back, just put a star on before I got arrested, that I wore a suit without a star.
I got married April the 1st, 1942.
And went into Kremboong April the 2nd.
April the 2nd, yeah.
I was supposed to go in April the 1st.
But then when I told them that I got married that day, on account of a pre-arrangement made two weeks prior, I got 24 hours, April the 2nd, first train out.
In Amsterdam.
With her father and mother.
Oh, she stayed with her family.
Yeah.
When I was in Kremboong, she visited me there.
It was not guarded by Germans, the camp in Holland.
No, no, no, no, no.
No, no, no, no, no.
It was a camp we were able to go in and out, without supervision or anything.
It was just a work camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the Dutch-- but the Dutch government runned it on orders of the Germans.
That's correct.
Now-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here I go again.
Anyway, I'm going to tell you to the best of my ability.
Up till today, I am a hard worker, very hard worker.
And that was noticeable.
I was young and strong, was 18 years old, 19 years old.
And I had also to dig in-- minuscule work, what it was.
I, for one, was assigned to dig the trenches to build a morgue.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was no picnic for me to do.
I was told.
I was told that they had to dig trenches.
They're going to build them a morgue.
